metadata.seed: I was doing okay, but, I mean, I got comfortable with that extra income, and so now my fear is that I've gotten too comfortable with it, and now that it's not there anymore, it's going to affect the fact I'm not going to be able to pay for food or my rent.

patient.query: I was doing okay, but, I mean, I got comfortable with that extra income, and so now my fear is that I've gotten too comfortable with it, and now that it's not there anymore, it's going to affect the fact I'm not going to be able to pay for food or my rent.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not going to be able to pay for food
I'm not going to be able to pay for rent

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that losing the extra income will leave you unable to cover food and rent. What evidence do you have that this will definitely happen? Are there ways you might still manage or plan to adjust your budget or seek support?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I just feel this weight, you know? It's like every time I think about losing that extra income, my mind goes straight to worst-case scenarios. I can’t shake the fear that I’ll fall behind on bills or end up struggling to put food on the table. I mean, I know there are some options, like budgeting better or asking family for help, but the thought of needing to do that feels really embarrassing... like I’m failing.

automatic_thoughts: worst-case scenarios
I’ll fall behind on bills
I’ll struggle to put food on the table
needing to budget better feels embarrassing
I’m failing

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy that worry feels, especially when it turns into a sense of failure. What evidence do you have that you’ll actually fall behind on bills or can’t provide food? If you could step back and imagine how a supportive friend might view this situation, what might they say?

patient.query: I don’t know... It just feels so real to me. I look at my bank account and see those numbers drop, and it’s like my heart just sinks. A friend would probably say I’m being too hard on myself or that I’m not seeing the whole picture. But in my mind, all I can focus on is the possibility of it actually happening. It's like I can almost picture it, and that makes everything feel so much heavier.

automatic_thoughts: it's actually happening
I'm being too hard on myself
I'm not seeing the whole picture

llm_response.response: It sounds like seeing the numbers drop feels very real and heavy for you. What evidence do you have that this will actually happen, and how have similar situations played out in the past? Are there other ways you might view this change that could feel less overwhelming?

patient.query: It’s just... I guess I’m really worried about how quickly things can change. I remember when I lost my last job and that spiral of stress—it was so hard to bounce back. Every time I open my account, I just can’t help but think back to that. It feels like a loop, seeing those numbers drop and imagining it leading to something worse. I want to think there might be a silver lining, but right now that feels pretty far away.

automatic_thoughts: things can change quickly
I can't bounce back
it will lead to something worse
there's no silver lining

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle of worry. What evidence do you have that the drop in numbers will inevitably lead to something worse, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? If you could step back and view the situation from a neutral perspective, what might you notice that could help you see a different outcome?

patient.query: I hear you, but when I’m in it, all I can see is that downward trend. It feels so certain to me. I mean, sure, there have been times where things worked out, but those moments feel like they’re buried under all this fear. Trying to look at it neutrally feels impossible when my mind races with all the “what ifs.” It’s like I’m stuck in this fog where everything seems risky, and I struggle to see a way out.

automatic_thoughts: everything seems risky
certain that things won't work out
good moments are buried
behaviors: struggling to see a way out

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that the trend is truly downward, and are there moments that might suggest otherwise? How might a different perspective on those “what ifs” change the picture you’re seeing right now? What small steps could you try that might help you feel less stuck in that fog?

patient.query:
I guess it’s just... the way things have felt lately, like every expense is piling up or how little savings I have left. But, when I think about it, there were times when I managed to save a bit or even got a bonus I wasn’t expecting. It’s hard to hold onto those moments, though, when the stress feels so overwhelming. 

As for the “what ifs,” I can kind of see how a more positive spin might help, but it’s tough.

automatic_thoughts: every expense is piling up
I have little savings
it's hard to hold onto positive moments

llm_response.response: It sounds like the financial pressures are weighing heavily, yet you still notice those moments when you unexpectedly saved or received a bonus. What might help you keep those moments in mind when the stress feels overwhelming? Perhaps we could explore ways to remind yourself of those successes during tough times.

patient.query: I mean, I guess acknowledging those moments could help, right? But I don’t really know how to do that. When the pressure hits, it’s like my brain just shuts down, and I forget all those little wins. Maybe if I wrote them down or something? But then I worry that it might feel forced, and it wouldn’t make a difference anyway. I just don’t want to feel like I’m grasping at straws.

automatic_thoughts: my brain just shuts down
I forget all those little wins
it might feel forced
it wouldn’t make a difference anyway
I’m grasping at straws

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that jotting down your wins might feel like a chore and not actually help. What evidence do you have that it wouldn’t make a difference? And if you did try it, how might you notice whether it changes how you feel when pressure hits?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: belief that writing wins is ineffective
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I haven’t really tried it, so I don’t know for sure. It’s just hard to think that writing it all down would actually change anything. When I’m feeling that pressure, it’s like all those wins fade away, and I'm just left with the anxiety. Maybe if I did try, I might catch myself remembering those moments? But... what if it just feels silly? I don’t want to waste my time on something that might not help.

automatic_thoughts: writing it all down won't change anything
all wins fade away
what if it just feels silly?
I don’t want to waste my time

llm_response.response: I hear you’re unsure whether jotting things down will make a difference. What evidence do you have that it won’t help, and can you think of any moments when writing or reflecting has shifted your perspective before? If you were to try it, what small, low‑stakes way might you test whether it brings back those wins you feel are fading?

patient.query: Yeah, I haven't really tried that. It just feels hard to think writing it down would change anything. When the pressure hits, everything good just fades away, and I’m left with that anxiety. I mean, maybe if I tried, I could remember those moments? But then I worry it would feel silly, and I don't want to waste time on something that might not help.

automatic_thoughts: writing it down wouldn't change anything
everything good fades away
maybe if I tried, I could remember those moments
it would feel silly
I don't want to waste time on something that might not help

llm_response.response:
**
Do you think there might be any small ways writing could help, even if it feels like it won’t change much? What evidence do you have that it would be a waste of time? How might you test that idea in a low‑stakes way?

patient.query: I don’t know... It just feels like such a big leap to think it could help. I mean, I could try it, I guess, just to see. But I keep thinking that my mind will just shut down again, and I won't know what to write. Maybe if I just started with one thing, like a good memory or something, that wouldn’t feel too overwhelming? But that brings up the fear of it not being enough or feeling dumb. I don’t want to add to

automatic_thoughts: my mind will just shut down again
I won't know what to write
it not being enough
feeling dumb
behaviors: thinking about starting with one thing

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that writing might feel like a failure or that your mind will just shut down. What evidence do you have that this will happen, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? If you start with a single memory, how could you decide what “enough” looks like for that one piece?
